response.description_long:
Modelgrid attacks the defining trade-off of serverless GPU inference: keep expensive GPUs warm around the clock, or accept cold starts of a minute or more while weights load. Its answer is a snapshot architecture — the fully-initialized model, including weights, CUDA context, and allocator state, is checkpointed into a distributed cache on NVMe in the same rack as the GPUs. Restoring a snapshot brings a model from zero to serving in under 2 seconds, even for models tens of gigabytes in size, and the site says snapshots are validated for models up to 140 GB.

The platform runs anything with a PyTorch or vLLM serving path — language models, embedding models, diffusion models, speech-to-text — brought as a container or a Python function, returning an HTTPS endpoint with token auth. Modelgrid handles batching, queueing, autoscaling from zero, and GPU selection across A100 and H100 classes, with multi-GPU serving on H100 nodes with NVLink for models that shard across devices. The scheduler watches queue depth against per-endpoint latency targets, a policy that only works because a restore costs 2 seconds rather than 2 minutes; restores are deterministic, so the tenth replica comes up as fast as the first.

The economics are per-second and compute-only: queue time and restore time are free, and scale-to-zero means an idle deployed model costs nothing. Rates run from $2.10 per hour equivalent for an A100 40 GB to $5.60 for an H100, billed by the second. The docs work the example: an embedding endpoint serving 10 minutes of traffic a day meters roughly 6 GPU-hours a month where an always-warm alternative meters 720. The intended customer is a team with spiky traffic — overnight batch jobs, consumer apps with regional peaks, agents that fan out to many models.

The company posture is owner-operator. Modelgrid runs its own capacity in three US regions, deploys endpoints across at least two, and publishes status and latency numbers openly, including restore-time p50 and p95 by model-size bucket and uptime history back to launch. Workloads run in isolated VMs with dedicated GPU attachment, snapshots are encrypted with per-tenant keys, and the platform is SOC 2 Type II certified.

The site names no customers and discloses no funding; scale has to be read from the infrastructure claims. Modelgrid was founded in San Francisco in 2023 by Ilya Sorokin and Grace Tan, infrastructure engineers from a large GPU cloud who watched customers pay for idle GPUs to avoid cold starts they could not fix — the founding bet being that cold starts are a consequence of replaying initialization that could have been saved.
